schools.summary: The three years' peace is dated from Ahab's rash covenant with Ben-hadad (1 Kings 20:34); Syria, recovered, still holds Ramoth-gilead.
Gill: the quiet was a reprieve of Elijah's sentence, respited three years for Ahab's humiliation in sackcloth.
Clarke: the peace ran from the treaty of 1 Kings 20:34, in which Ramoth-gilead should have been restored but was not.
Henry: an enemy's promises, like his kisses, are deceitful; Ahab has a good cause yet fails, for equity is not judged by prosperity.
The visit rests on a marriage alliance (2 Chronicles 18:1); Ahab turns a festive occasion into a war council over Ramoth-gilead.
Gill: it was in the third year of the peace, before it expired, that Jehoshaphat came down.
Ramoth-gilead was a city of refuge and frontier fortress long lost to Syria; the 400 prophets were court prophets of the calf-worship, neither Baal's men nor true prophets of the Lord.
Gill: Ahab 'had said' this to his council before Jehoshaphat's arrival — the design was already fixed.
Ahab, weak in forces, seeks Judah's aid; Jehoshaphat's answer is one of deference to the stronger kingdom.
Clarke: the alliance sprang from the Jehoram-Athaliah marriage and displeased God, drawing Jehu's rebuke.
Henry: too great a complaisance toward evildoers drew a good king into danger; piety cannot sanctify a chosen unequal yoke.
Gill: an auxiliary as strong as Judah would greatly serve Ahab's small forces.
Gill: a pious prince would not go to war without first inquiring of the Lord by his prophets, and would have it done at once.
Henry: a good man takes God and his religion with him everywhere, unashamed to own it even among those who despise it.
The 400 were neither true prophets of Jehovah nor open Baalists, but court prophets of Jeroboam's calf-worship, borrowing the Lord's name (v.12).
Gill: false prophets of the groves, spared from Jezebel's table when Baal's prophets were slain.
Clarke: prophets of Asherah maintained by Jezebel, absent from the trial on Carmel.
Henry: they stole Jehovah's name to please Jehoshaphat and said 'Go up' to please Ahab; unanimity is no mark of truth.
Ellicott: the prophets used only Adonai; Jehoshaphat, noting their shrinking from the covenant name Jehovah, asks for a prophet who dares speak in it.
Gill: Jehoshaphat suspected them false but would not say so to Ahab's face, and asks for another.
Henry: exercised spiritual senses discern the counterfeit; one faithful prophet was worth all four hundred.
Micaiah ('who is like Jehovah') is likely the prophet who rebuked Ahab over Ben-hadad (1 Kings 20:35) and was imprisoned, explaining Ahab's grudge.
Henry: Ahab's hatred of the plain-dealing prophet is the mark of a hardened sinner; he may thank himself for the evil word.
Gill: but one true prophet remained in Samaria, Elijah and Elisha being elsewhere.
The 'officer' is a saris, a court chamberlain or eunuch (1 Samuel 8:15); Ahab commands haste — 'bring quickly.'
Clarke: such a servant was likely a foreigner, since an Israelite could not lawfully be so degraded.
Henry: Ahab knows where to find the prophet because he lies in prison, yet the word of the Lord is not bound.
The kings sit robed on a threshing-floor before the gate; the display of pomp and prophetic frenzy is staged to overawe Micaiah.
Gill: the two kings sat in great state and majesty, robed, at the entrance of the gate.
Symbolic sign-acts and the horn as emblem of strength are thoroughly biblical (Deut. 33:17; 1 Sam. 2:1; Dan. 8:3); Zedekiah apes a genuine form.
Gill: horns of iron signify power and might; true prophets also used such visible signs.
Clarke: this imitates prophecy that instructed by significant actions, frequent among the Lord's prophets.
Ellicott: the prophets grow bold enough to use Jehovah's name; their mounting clamour recalls the 'O Baal, hear us' of Carmel.
Gill: all the prophets alike encouraged the king and promised victory.
Henry: unity is not always the mark of a true ministry — four hundred with one mouth, and all in error.
Ellicott: the officer embodies the superstition that believes in inspiration yet thinks it can be bribed or coerced — the error of Simon Magus and of Balak.
Clarke: what notion of prophecy is it that supposes the prophet can model his prediction to fit the king?
Henry: the officer judged Micaiah by himself, a man-pleaser hoping for preferment; worldlings think all others as worldly as they.
K&D: Micaiah answers the road-side pressure with a solemn oath to speak only what Jehovah says to him.
Gill: he swore by the living God to confirm the message he was about to deliver.
Henry: he will speak God's word without addition, diminution, or alteration, his eye on a King higher than the two before him.
Henry: Micaiah echoes the flatterers by way of derision — those who love to be flattered deserve to be bantered; Ahab's adjuration is itself a pretence.
Clarke: the oracle is deliberately ambiguous ('deliver it into the king's hand'), like the double-tongued heathen oracles to Croesus and Pyrrhus, which Micaiah mockingly throws back.
Ellicott: Micaiah is a true disciple of Elijah in the defiant irony with which he mocks the false prophets and the king.
K&D: from the words and the tone Ahab perceived the irony and adjured Micaiah to speak only truth in Jehovah's name — proof he can tell true from false.
Gill: Ahab adjures him now 'once for all,' not that he had done so repeatedly before.
Clarke: the sheep without a shepherd foretell Israel's defeat and Ahab's death — the political shepherd shall fall in battle.
Henry: smite the shepherd and the sheep scatter (Zech. 13:7); yet the Lord sends the people home in peace — a dishonourable retreat, not a slaughter.
K&D: though Ahab asked for a true word, he tries to blame the unfavourable prophecy on Micaiah's personal enmity.
Henry: those who bear malice believe others bear malice to them; but to warn a man of his danger is the greatest kindness, not spite.
Gill: Ahab plainly perceives the prophet has foretold that he should fall in battle.

themes: the kindness of a hard truth

schools.summary: Clarke: a parable telling in figurative language what was in the womb of providence — Micaiah softens 'thy prophets are liars' into a vision.
Gill: things are brought down to the capacities of men, not really transacted; the vision discloses the hidden ground of the false prophecy.
Henry: the Lord on his throne is a King above all kings; the affairs of earth, and the fall of Ahab, are governed from heaven.
Gill: not a real consultation with angels, but the settled decree that Ahab should fall at Ramoth by the man he had spared — a just punishment.
K&D: the heavenly council is the divinely-chosen way to reveal that the prophets' unanimity is the appointed instrument of judgment.
Gill: the volunteer is not one of the holy host, who cannot lie, but Satan himself, the father of lies, come forth from his own place.
Ellicott: hesitates to import 'the Satan'; reads 'the spirit' as a power of evil overruled to work God's judgments, whose subordination precludes dualism.

debates.question: What is 'the spirit' that volunteers to deceive Ahab?

sides.position: Satan, the father of lies, present as in Job's prologue
a subordinate spiritual power of evil, overruled by God, not to be flatly named 'the Satan'

schools.summary: Clarke: 'Go forth, and do so' means God permits the lying spirit; Ahab, fairly warned, forsook the counsels of truth for those of falsity — his blood on his own head.
Gill: the Lord's 'Wherewith?' is not ignorance but a way to draw out and expose the scheme.
Clarke: Scripture represents God as doing what he only permits; nothing is done but by his energy or permission, so he can 'put' the lie without being the liar.
Henry: represented after the manner of men — God is not the author of sin, but gives up to strong delusion those who love not the truth (2 Thess. 2:11).
Gill: the Lord suffered the lying spirit and sent strong delusions, working through minds already bent to flatter Ahab.
Ellicott: the emphatic double 'the LORD' answers Ahab's charge of mere malice in vv.8, 18 — this is Jehovah's own word.

themes: God not the author of sin

schools.summary: Ellicott: the blow feigns indignation at blasphemy, the pretext later used against Christ and Paul (John 18:22; Acts 23:2); Micaiah answers only with a prophecy of shame.
Clarke: the taunt claims a monopoly of the Spirit — 'was it at this ear that it entered to speak to thee?' — insolence matching the assault.
Henry: false prophets are the worst enemies of the true; Micaiah, like the smitten Judge of Israel, does not return the blow.
Gill: Zedekiah stepped forward in haste and passion.
Gill: Zedekiah would flee to an inner chamber after the defeat, fearing the enemy or the people who would blame the prophets.
Clarke: he likely hid from Jezebel and the courtiers after the disaster, and there probably perished.
Henry: those who will not be corrected in time by the word of God are undeceived, too late, by his judgments.
Ellicott: Micaiah is remanded to Amon the governor and Joash the king's son, the latter perhaps left in charge of Samaria, as with Ahab's sons in 2 Kings 10:1.
Gill: the order to 'carry him back' shows Micaiah returned to a confinement he had not left.
Ellicott: the harsh sentence (cf. Isa. 30:20) and 'until I come in peace' betray affected, not real, disbelief; Micaiah likely later paid with his life.
Clarke: bread and water of affliction strip him of every comfort, keeping him alive only to witness Ahab's supposed triumph.
Gill: the common prison, perhaps a close cell of 'little ease,' where the prophet had been before.
K&D: 'people' means the nations, summoned as witnesses (cf. Micah 1:2); Ellicott notes the phrase opens the Book of Micah and may be an early interpolation.
Gill: Micaiah is content to be reckoned a false prophet and punished if Ahab returns alive.
Henry: he ran no hazard, for he knew whom he believed; God will confirm his servants' word (Isa. 44:26).
Jehoshaphat, held by false shame and a virtual dependence on Israel sealed by the marriage alliance, goes up and is rebuked by Jehu (2 Chron. 19:2).
Henry: his pious but easy temper let him be carried away by Ahab's prophets, as Barnabas by dissimulation (Gal. 2:13).
Gill: the prophets' agreement, his given word, and Ahab's just cause, together with Micaiah's word touching Ahab alone, inclined him to go.
Ellicott: the disguise is Ahab's half-belief and half-unbelief, absurdly hoping to avert a pronounced judgment yet true to a nature 'halting between two opinions'; K&D hear anxiety in the grammar.
Clarke: the LXX reads 'put on MY robes'; Jehoshaphat may have worn Ahab's, so the Syrians mistook him — a treacherous precaution.
Gill: the disguise, whether common dress or a coat of mail, was a device to elude Micaiah's prophecy.
The 32 captains are ordered to strike only at the king of Israel; recovered Syria aims its whole force at the person of the king who had spared it.
Henry: Ahab is justly repaid for sparing Ben-hadad, who stung the bosom that spared him (cf. 1 Kings 20:42).
Gill: the thirty-two captains match the number of the earlier Syrian wars, now leading the divisions of the host.
Ellicott: the cry rallies his people; the Chronicler adds 'the Lord helped him, and God moved them to depart' (2 Chron. 18:31).
Henry: the danger rebukes the confederacy ('I am as thou art'), the rescue shows God had not deserted him; his cry was devotion, not cowardice.
Gill: seen in royal robes, Jehoshaphat was taken for the king of Israel.

themes: the cry of a good man

schools.summary: K&D: at his cry the captains perceive their mistake and turn aside, having pressed on him while he seemed the king of Israel.
Gill: bound to fight the king of Israel only, they let Jehoshaphat go once they knew him.

themes: reprieve by the enemy's own order

schools.summary: Ellicott: tradition names the archer Naaman; the war-chariot holds two, this being its first mention in actual battle; Clarke weighs lethummo ('at a venture').
Henry: the random shaft hit the right man in the one vulnerable joint — no armour is proof against divine vengeance; the casual is the counsel of God.
Gill: the archer aimed at no one, yet God directed the arrow to the man marked for destruction, past disguise and mail.
K&D and Ellicott: the king holds himself upright to hearten his men and bleeds to death by evening; a desperate bravery joined to moral cowardice, true to nature.
Clarke: he hid his wound lest his troops be discouraged.
Henry: propped up to watch the battle, he had time to feel himself die, reflecting in horror on Baal, Naboth, and the imprisoned prophet.

themes: the vision fulfilled before his eyes

schools.summary: K&D: toward sunset the cry runs through the host, 'Every one to his city and his land.'
Clarke: with Ahab dead, both sides consent to end the war; Israel and Syria go home and Ramoth is left.
Gill: the proclamation, near the hour of Ahab's death, was made by the king or by Jehoshaphat.
K&D: the terse Hebrew means the king reached Samaria dead — he who went out promising 'until I come in peace' returns a corpse.
The dogs licking the blood fulfils Elijah's word (1 Kings 21:19); Ellicott follows the LXX 'the harlots bathed' over KJV 'washed his armour,' and weighs the Jezreel difficulty (2 Kings 9:25).
Henry: Naboth's blood avenged and David's word fulfilled (Ps. 68:23); the dogs made no difference between royal and common blood.
Gill: the driver washed the blood-stained chariot in the pool of Samaria.

debates.question: Does the clause read 'they washed his armour' or 'the harlots bathed' in the pool?

sides.position: 'the harlots bathed' — the LXX and Josephus reading, the natural sense of the Hebrew
'they washed his armour' — the received English rendering

schools.summary: Ellicott: ivory reappears for the first time since Solomon (1 Kings 10:18), a Zidonian art fitting Ahab's link with Jezebel; his reign was outwardly prosperous, its corruption not yet ripened.
Gill: a palace inlaid with ivory (cf. Ps. 45:8), against which Amos would later cry (Amos 3:15).
K&D: 'slept with his fathers' is used even of Ahab's violent end; the historian turns to summarize Jehoshaphat's reign.
Gill: more of Ahaziah follows in this chapter and the next book.
Ellicott: the book centres on Elijah and Elisha in Israel, inserting only brief annals of Judah; the full record of Jehoshaphat is in the Chronicler (2 Chron. 17-20).
Henry: one of the best reigns, not the longest; its single blemish the intimacy with the house of Ahab in war and trade.
Gill: beginning in Ahab's fourth year, he reigned about eighteen years with Ahab and seven after him.

themes: a good king contrasted with Ahab

schools.summary: K&D: the same regnal figures stand in the Chronicler's summary (2 Chron. 20:31); the verdict is that he walked in the way of Asa.
Gill: thirty-five at accession and twenty-five years reigning make him sixty at his death.
Ellicott: the key is 'and the groves' — Jehoshaphat swept away the idolatrous (Asherah) high places but left the unauthorized Jehovah-sanctuaries (cf. 2 Chron. 20:33).
Clarke: distinguish idolatrous high places (removed) from those consecrated to the Lord (left); or read 2 Chron. 17:6 as 'did not take away.'
Gill and K&D: he walked wholly in Asa's good way, yet left the old corruption of the high places standing.

debates.question: How can the high places be 'not taken away' here yet 'taken away' in 2 Chronicles 17:6?

sides.position: two kinds of high places — idolatrous ones removed, irregular Jehovah-sanctuaries left
a textual variant — the Chronicler may have read 'did not take away'

schools.summary: K&D: peace with Israel was laudable in itself, but Jehoshaphat went beyond bounds by the marriage alliance with Ahab's house (2 Chron. 18:1); Ellicott notes the verse is chronologically out of place.
Gill: peace first with Ahab, sealed by affinity and the joint war, then with Ahaziah.
K&D: his 'might' includes fortifications, army, and above all instruction in the law and reform of justice (2 Chron. 17:7; 19:4), as well as his wars.
Gill: many of these acts are preserved in Chronicles; the annals held more than the Spirit chose to transmit.
Clarke: the qedeshim are 'consecrated persons' of a polluted cult; Jehoshaphat destroyed the whole system of ritual filth (cf. 1 Kings 14:24; 15:12).
Gill: Asa removed many; his son, of like disposition, removed the remnant that remained.
Ellicott and Clarke: Edom, subdued since David, was ruled by a deputy till Jehoram; the note prepares for the fleet at the Edomite port of Ezion-geber.
Gill: Edom's government by a viceroy under Judah is noted to explain how Jehoshaphat could build ships at Ezion-geber.

themes: the ground laid for the fleet

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott: the fleet, a joint Judah-Israel venture denounced by Eliezer (2 Chron. 20:37), was wrecked by a storm — judgment on the alliance with Ahab's house.
Gill: 'ships of Tarshish' are sea-going vessels, ten by one reading, meant for the gold of Ophir.
Clarke: Jehoshaphat 'joined himself to Ahaziah' (2 Chron. 20:36); 'ships of Tarshish' may mean ships of burden.
Henry: the rod of the shipwreck, expounded by the word, broke Jehoshaphat off from the ungodly alliance — a fool learns by experience what the word had already taught.
K&D: after the wreck, Jehoshaphat refused Ahaziah's fresh proposal, having seen Eliezer's prophecy fulfilled.
Clarke: perhaps a second proposal, or (following Houbigant) the Hebrew should read 'he consented' rather than 'he would not.'

debates.question: Did Jehoshaphat refuse Ahaziah, or (by emendation) consent?

sides.position: he refused — the received text and the moral of a chastened king
he consented — Houbigant's conjectural emendation of a single Hebrew letter

schools.summary: Henry: his death shuts up his story (though he is named again, 2 Kings 3:7); better to die in one's honour than outlive it, as Jehoshaphat did with unbroken integrity.
Gill: buried in the city of David with David, Rehoboam, Abijam, and Asa — gathered to the godly line.
Ellicott: Jezebel's influence revives in the short reign, Baal-worship resuming beside Jeroboam's calves and Baalzebub (2 Kings 1:2); Gill resolves the chronological knot by a co-regency.
Henry: a short, bad reign; Ahaziah took no warning from his father's ruin, following his wicked father and more wicked mother.
Gill: the date is squared by supposing Ahaziah crowned in his father's lifetime, before Ramoth.
K&D: the threefold way of father, mother, and Jeroboam gathers both the calf-worship and the Baal-cult (1 Kings 16:30); the chapter and book close unsuitably here, mid-reign.
Gill: the evil he did was idolatry.

themes: idolatry walking on in the son

schools.summary: K&D: the closing sentence seals Ahaziah's character, noting expressly, as of Ahab (1 Kings 16:31), that he served and worshipped Baal.
Gill: he served Baal as his father had done and his mother still did.

themes: the son the image of the father
provoking the LORD God of Israel
